content.problemStatement:
Let X be the set of all integers between 0 and n-1. Suppose we have a collection S1, S2, ..., Sm of subsets of X. Say an atom A is a subset of X such that for each Si we have either A is a subset of Si or A and Si do not have any common elements.



Your task is to find a collection A1, ..., Ak of atoms such that every item in X is in some Ai and no two Ai, Aj with i ≠ j share a common item. Surely such a collection exists as we could create a single set {x} for each x in X. A more interesting question is to minimize k, the number of atoms.
constraints: All elements are between 0 and n‑1
Each element of X belongs to at least one Si
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer t (1 ≤ t ≤ 30), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers n and m (1 ≤ n ≤ 100, 1 ≤ m ≤ 30). Then follow m lines. The i‑th line begins with vi (1 ≤ vi ≤ n) – the size of set Si – followed by vi distinct integers in the range [0, n‑1] describing the elements of Si. Every integer from 0 to n‑1 appears in at least one Si.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the minimum possible number of atoms into which X can be partitioned.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case: masks are {0,1}:{001}, {2}:{011}, {3,4}:{010}. Three distinct masks → 3 atoms. Second test case: each element obtains a unique mask, so 4 atoms.
Masks: element 0 → 1, element 1 → 0, element 2 → 1. Distinct masks are {0,1}, so the minimum number of atoms is 2.
Masks: 0 and 1 have 01, 2 and 3 have 10. Two different masks → 2 atoms.

solution.approach: For every element x in X we build a binary mask of length m. Bit i (0‑based) is 1 iff x belongs to Si. Two elements can be placed in the same atom exactly when their masks are identical, because an atom must be either completely inside a set or completely outside it. Therefore the minimum number of atoms equals the number of distinct masks among the n elements. The algorithm:  • Initialise an array mask[0…n‑1] with 0.  • For each set Si (i from 0 to m‑1) read its elements and set the i‑th bit in mask[x] for every x in Si.  • Insert all mask values into a hash set (or sort and count unique values).  • The size of the set is the answer. The procedure is repeated for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O( Σ vi ) per test case, i.e. O(n·m) in the worst case because each element can appear in every set. With the given limits this is at most 3000 operations per case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the mask array plus O(n) for the set of distinct masks.
